headline: October's Ancient Song Returns to the Diamond
subheadline: As the playoff curtain rises, the Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs and Hiroshima Radiation remind us why autumn baseball carries the weight of cathedral bells.
body:
There is a moment in every October when the game strips itself of all pretense and reveals its truest self—raw, unforgiving, eternal. We arrived at such a moment this week, as the opening round of playoff baseball unfolded across our league like a William Faulkner novel set between the chalk lines, full of sound and fury, signifying everything.

Consider, if you will, the **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs**, who navigated the most treacherous of waters against the **Albuquerque Bombers** in a 5.0–4.0 affair that will be whispered about in dugouts for years to come. This was baseball played on a knife's edge, the kind of game that recalls the 1991 World Series, when Kirby Puckett told the world he would see them tomorrow and then made certain of it. The Bombers, who had spent the regular season building toward this crescendo, found themselves undone not by collapse but by the Wonderdogs' refusal to yield. Sometimes the margin between October glory and winter's cold silence is no wider than a single breath.

**Hiroshima Radiation** offered no such drama, instead delivering a 9.0–1.0 dismantling of the **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** that carried the cold efficiency of Bob Gibson in his 1968 prime. There was no suspense, only execution—the kind of performance that makes opposing managers stare at their lineup cards as if the answers might rearrange themselves into something more hopeful. The Twins, for all their regular season promise, were made to look like men swinging at shadows.

Meanwhile, the **Sugar Land Skeeters** authored their own statement, dispatching the **Canberra Wombats** 7.0–3.0 with the quiet confidence of a team that has been here before and fully intends to be here again. In the consolation rounds, the **Riverside Roughnecks** posted the week's most lopsided victory, overwhelming **North Correa** 9.0–1.0, a performance that suggested they may have arrived at their true form too late, though the baseball gods have been known to reward such late-blooming excellence.

The standings reset themselves for the playoff bracket, as they must, democracy giving way to tournament monarchy. Three teams remain in the championship hunt: the Skeeters, the Wonderdogs, and Hiroshima Radiation, each carrying their own October narratives forward.

Next week, the survivors advance toward immortality. The Skeeters and Wonderdogs appear destined for collision, while Hiroshima Radiation awaits, patient as winter. Somewhere, a championship banner hangs unclaimed, waiting to learn its owner's name.
